paper_title: Neighbors and the Evolution of the Comparative Advantage of Nations: Evidence of International Knowledge Diffusion?
research_answer: Yes — the neighbor effect operates on both the extensive margin (adding new products) and the intensive margin (growing existing ones). For products a country already exports, having a neighbor with a revealed comparative advantage (RCA above 1) in that product is associated with annual RCA growth of 1.5%–1.7%, which compounds to a 16–18% cumulative increase over a decade. In nominal export value terms, the effect is even larger: having a neighbor that exports a product is correlated with 4%–5% higher annual export value growth for that product. So neighbors don't just help countries discover new export opportunities — they also accelerate the deepening of existing comparative advantages.
supporting_quotes: For existing products, having a neighbor with comparative advantage in them is associated with a growth of exports that is higher by 1.5 percent per annum.
Panel B shows that having a neighbor with RCA>1 is associated with a future annual growth of RCA of 1.5%-1.7% for that product in the next ten year period (or 16 - 18% cumulative).
Having a neighbor that exports a product is correlated with an increase in the annual growth of exports for that product of 4% to 5%.

data_points.metric: Additional annual RCA growth from neighbor with RCA>1
Additional annual export value growth from neighbor with RCA>1
